Background

I bridge biomedical research and data analytics by turning complex, imperfect scientific data into structured evidence. My focus is not only building models, but making the full workflow reliable: defining the problem, cleaning the data, designing reproducible pipelines, evaluating baselines, and explaining what the result can actually support.

My background across psychology, neuroscience, wet-lab research, clinical research exposure, and applied computational science helps me work between domains. I can understand biological questions, translate them into data problems, and build outputs that are useful for researchers, clinicians, and product-facing teams.

Project 01 · Deployed prototype
Biomedical Provenance Review Console
An evidence-backed review console for classifying whether biomedical papers mainly use author-generated data or reused public datasets. The system combines accession evidence extraction, RAG retrieval, optional Ollama local/cloud review, and Supabase-based feedback collection.
FastAPI + JavaScript RAG-grounded LLM review Supabase feedback loop
Evidence-first classificationExtracts GSE/accession evidence and classifies papers as Primary or Reuse using baseline models and RAG-derived signals.
RAG-grounded reviewer layerOptional local or cloud Ollama review receives retrieved labeled examples, but high-confidence baseline decisions remain locked.
Curated feedback to RAG updateReviewer corrections are stored as pending feedback, manually approved, exported, and merged into a refreshed RAG bank.
Open live demo GitHub Feedback → RAG workflow

Public demo uses demo-safe data. The LLM reviewer is advisory, and public feedback is staged for manual review before it can update the RAG bank.

Embedded live demo Render
If the embedded demo is slow, Render may be waking from free-tier sleep. Open the demo in a new tab for the best experience.
Deployment status Public prototype
Project 02 · Featured thesis case study

OriGENE: deep learning from regulatory signal

OriGENE investigates whether the shape of promoter-centered H3K4me3 ChIP-seq signal can help identify cancer-driver genes. The case study combines biological representation, a 1D CNN with Inception-style feature learning, a mutation-aware extension, and matched benchmarks against mutation- and epigenetic-based baselines.

Explore the case study Back to selected projects
Biological contextdriver genes and dysregulation
Best AUROC
0.786
OriGENE Epi+Mut
Best AUPRC
0.800
OriGENE Epi+Mut
External transfer
0.736–0.773
colorectal AUROC
Input
H3K4me3
1D promoter signal

Case overview

Most cancer-driver prediction methods rely heavily on mutation-derived evidence. OriGENE tests a complementary hypothesis: cancer-driver genes may also be recognized from abnormal regulatory activity around their transcription start sites.

Biological question

Can H3K4me3 promoter signal distinguish cancer driver genes from neutral genes, even when mutation signals are sparse or incomplete?

Model input

The main input is a one-dimensional H3K4me3 enrichment profile centered on each gene, converted into fixed-length binned arrays.

Model design

The CNN reads local and broader peak-shape patterns through Conv1D layers and Inception-style modules. Mutation features enter as a separate tabular branch.

Evaluation principle

Models were compared under matched genes, datasets, cross-validation settings, and metrics to avoid overstating performance.

Core result
0.786 AUROC
Best benchmark performance from OriGENE with epigenetic and mutation features.
Main interpretation
Regulatory signal matters
The epigenetic-only model remained close to the combined model, suggesting H3K4me3 carries strong predictive information.
Generalization
0.736–0.773
AUROC range in external colorectal transfer experiments.
Workflowredrawn scientific pipeline
End-to-end workflow from public ChIP-seq data to gene-centered signal arrays, model training, mutation-feature integration, and transfer benchmarking.
Preprocessing
ChIP-seq → hg38 → bedGraph
Raw H3K4me3 sequencing signal is aligned and converted into genome-wide coverage tracks.
Representation
TSS-centered H3K4me3 arrays
Each gene is represented as a fixed-length 1D signal window around the transcription start site.
Evaluation
Same genes, splits, and metrics
OriGENE is compared with DORGE and 20/20+ under matched benchmark settings.
OriGENE CNN architectureepigenetic signal + mutation branch
The architecture summarizes the final portfolio model view: a 1D H3K4me3 signal is converted into a binned sequence, processed by Conv1D and Inception-style blocks, then merged with mutation-derived tabular features before producing a gene-level cancer-driver probability.

Epigenetic pathway

H3K4me3 is treated as an ordered genomic signal, so the model learns from peak shape, intensity, and local sequence context.

Mutation pathway

The 28 mutation-derived features are tabular inputs. They modulate the CNN representation instead of replacing the epigenetic signal.

Output

The sigmoid layer gives a gene-level probability: a high score means the gene is more likely to act as a cancer driver.

Benchmarkfinal project data
Benchmark overview: OriGENE is compared with DORGE and 20/20+ using matched data, splits, and metrics.
Best overall
OriGENE Epi+Mut
AUROC 0.786 · AUPRC 0.800. Combined regulatory and mutation features gave the strongest benchmark result.
Key observation
H3K4me3 is predictive
The epigenetic-only model remained close to the combined model, so regulatory signal is not just a minor auxiliary feature.
Baseline contrast
Mutation-only lower
Mutation features are useful, but mutation-only baselines did not capture all driver-gene evidence in this matched setting.
Transfer testingfinal project data
Transfer evaluation: cross-patient and colorectal transfer test whether learned promoter-signal patterns generalize beyond one sample context.
Cross-patient, 5 kbp
Lung II
AUC 0.74 · AUPRC 0.71 Liver I
AUC 0.74 · AUPRC 0.71 Liver II
AUC 0.73 · AUPRC 0.70 NT2-D1
AUC 0.64 · AUPRC 0.62
External colorectal transfer, 40 kbp
AUROC range
0.736–0.773 AUPRC range
0.712–0.762
Mutation feature signalredrawn reference figure
Mutation-feature interpretation: feature importance is shown as supporting context for the mutation branch.
Top mutation signal
Nonsilent / silent ratio
A compact indicator of whether observed mutations are likely to alter protein function.
Secondary signals
Missense · LoF · PolyPhen
These summarize mutation burden and predicted functional impact at gene level.
Role in OriGENE
Contextual support
Mutation features modulate the CNN representation; the main input remains promoter-centered H3K4me3 signal.
Project 03
Typhoon Day-Off Predictor
Modeling collective human decision-making under environmental risk. The project combines weather observations, typhoon features, imputation, scaling, and Random Forest inference, and now includes a bilingual interactive Taiwan map demo.

This iframe runs the deployed Render demo. It includes benchmark/live switching, a real Taiwan county map, and township-level area selection.

Open demo GitHub

Click each stage for details.

$ python app.py
-> Flask endpoint /predict -> county / district probability output for the demo UI

From MMscaler.joblib, trained on approximately 12,466 typhoon records.

Project 04
Alzheimer's Diagnosis ML
Identifying behavioral and cognitive indicators of Alzheimer's disease in 2,149 patients, comparing clustering, tree-based, and neural network approaches for clinical decision support.
Elbow Method
Silhouette Score

Elbow at k=7. Best silhouette at k=2 (score = 0.146). Final model uses k=7.

Architecture: 32 to 16 to 8 to 1, Dropout 0.2 x 3, Adam, EarlyStopping patience=5, stopped at epoch 31.

Project 05
Monte Carlo - Lennard-Jones Fluids
Physics simulation of molecular fluid behavior using Metropolis Monte Carlo, modeling phase transitions and structural properties relevant to biophysical systems.

U(r) = 4e[(s/r)^12 - (s/r)^6], e=1.05, s=1.0, minimum at r approximately 1.12s.

Energy per particle converges after roughly 100k iterations, with 500k NVT steps total.

g(r) averaged over 10 NVT snapshots, first shell peak at r approximately 1.1s, liquid phase rho = 0.74.

N particles
1,000
rho liquid
0.74

0.023
epsilon
1.05
sigma
1.0
T* = kT/e
~ 0.952
delta max displacement
0.5

500,000

1,000,000
r cutoff
r^2 >= 2.5
boundary

Project 06
FASTA Parser & MSA Scorer
Bioinformatics pipeline for parsing genetic data and scoring pairwise sequence alignments, enabling phylogenetic analysis of mtDNA and Y-chromosome variation across individuals.
Two-part workflow

Part 1 - FastaParser.py
Reads a cp950-encoded genetic data file and writes two FASTA files, one for mtDNA and one for Y chromosome.

Part 2 - MSA.py
Reads any aligned FASTA file and scores every pairwise alignment using match, gap, transition, and transversion weights.

Scoring parameters
KeyScoreDescription
match+1Identical bases
gap-1Insertion / deletion
transition-1A to G or C to T
transversion-2A/G to C/T
GitHub
StepCodePurpose
1 Readopen(infile, encoding='cp950', errors='replace')Handles legacy CJK-encoded files
2 Normalizereplace('\n\n\n', '\n\n')Consistent paragraph separators
3 Splitdata.strip().split('\n\n')One paragraph equals one individual
4 Parselines[0]=ID; scan for label; lines[i+1]=seqExtracts ID and sequence
5 Output>ID\nSEQ\n into mtDNA.fna + Y.fnaStandard FASTA format, UTF-8
$ python FastaParser.py GeneticData_1.txt mtDNA.fna Y.fna
$ python FastaParser.py GeneticData_1.txt # uses default filenames
StepDetail
1 argparse-f fasta -p params -o output with all flags documented
2 ValidateFile-not-found, empty-file check, invalid nucleotide counter
3 Read FASTABuild dic_seq = {ID: sequence}; allowed A C G T - ?; skip duplicates and invalids
4 ScoreO(n^2) all-pairs loop with alignment_score and alignment_identity_p
5 OutputSampleA SampleB IdentityScore Score as tab-separated TSV
$ python MSA.py -f mtDNA.fna -p parameter.txt -o scores.txt
# Output: SampleA SampleB IdentityScore Score

Live alignment_score() and alignment_identity_p() from MSA.py, reproduced in-browser.

A Match A Transition A Transversion - Gap
